Facts.text: The epoch (origin) of the modern Hebrew calendar (Proleptic Julian calendar)
Pope Saint Mark's death ends his reign as Catholic Pope leaving the papacy vacant
Battle of La Motta: A combined army from Spain and the Holy Roman Empire led by Ramón de Cardona defeat the Venetians at Schio
1st public burning of books in the Netherlands, in Louvain
British & Dutch troops under Marlborough occupy Roermond
People riot due to beer tax in Alkmaar, Netherlands
A cyclone causes 40-foot waves that are believed to have killed 300,000 in Calcutta, India
Stamp Act Congress (First Congress of the American Colonies) convenes in New York City to devise a unified protest against new British taxation
Americans beat British in Second Battle of Saratoga & Battle of Bemis Heights
British defeated by American militia near Kings Mountain, SC
Carbon paper patented in London by inventor Ralph Wedgwood
First double-decked steamboat, Washington, arrives in New Orleans
Miramichi Fire - forest fire disaster in New Brunswick destroys Douglastown and Newcastle, killing 200 to 500 people
Granite Railway (1st chartered railway in US) begins operations
The Greek city of Patras is liberated by the French expeditionary force in Peloponnese under General Maison.
The Black Line begins; a leveé of colonists in Tasmania, Australia, attempts to round up Aborigines onto the Tasman Peninsula
Willem I resigns as king of Netherlands
Cyrus Chambers Jr patents folding machine that folds book & newspapers
-Oct 13th) Battle of Darbytown Road, Virginia
Naval Engagement at Bahia Harbor, Brazil - CSS Florida vs USS Wachusett
Cornell University (Ithaca NY) opens
Leon Gambetta flees Paris in balloon
16-hour fire injures 30 of Chicago's 185 firefighters
Germany & Austrian-Hungary sign Twofold Covenant
Spain abolishes slavery in Cuba
The term "orienteering" is first used for an event
Crete revolts against Turkey & aligns with Greece
Serbia & Montenegro sign anti-Austria-Hungarian pact
British Security Service, commonly known as MI5 (Military Intelligence, Section 5), is formed as part of the Secret Service Bureau
The Helsinki Stock Exchange sees its first transaction.
The German submarine U-53 arrives off Newport, Rhode Island, and sinks 9 British merchant ships in international waters
KLM (Koninklijke Luchtvaart Maatschappij), Royal Dutch Airlines, is founded in Amsterdam, one of the world's oldest airlines
Oud-burgem of Rotterdam Zimmerman becomes High Comm's of Austria
Yankees Everett Scott runs his consecutive-game streak to 1,138
Greek government of Dikalekopoulis forms
Actress Theo Mann-Master resigns from stage
Italian Great Fascist Council forms
Race Tafari Makonnen crowned King of Abyssinia
1st infra-red photograph, Rochester, NY
Johan Wagenaar's "Feestmars" premieres in Amsterdam
Germany requires all Jewish passports be stamped with letter J
World War II: the McCollum memo proposes bringing the U.S. into the war in Europe by provoking the Japanese to attack the United States.
German army occupies Viarma, USSR
1 salvo Katjoesja-rocket destroys Nazi battalion in Stalingrad
Last camouflaged German raider Komet leaves Flushing (Netherlands)
US & UK government announce establishment of United Nations
Weill, Perelman and Nash' musical "One Touch of Venus" premieres in NYC
Allies bombs sea dikes at Vlissingen
Riots in Amersfoort/Utrecht/Strugle
Dutch author A M de Jong's murderer Ton van Gog escapes
German Democratic Republic formed from Russian occupation zone (National Day). Wilhelm Pieck becomes 1st president, Otto Greatwohl 1st premier
US forces invade North Korea by crossing 38th parallel
Walter Bedell Smith replaces Roscoe H Hillenkoetter as 4th CIA head
William H Jackson becomes deputy director of CIA
Hassan el Hodeiby, leader of the Muslim Brothership, arrested in Egypt
Aircraft carrier USS Saratoga launched at Brooklyn
KOAC TV channel 7 in Corvallis, OR (PBS) begins broadcasting
Potter Stewart appointed to US Supreme Court
US manned space-flight project renamed Project Mercury
TV series "Route 66" premieres on CBS
USSR performs nuclear test at Novaya Zemlya USSR
Bobby Baker resigns as Senate Democratic secretary
Hurricane Flora hits Haiti & Dominican Republic, kills 7,190
JFKsigns ratification for nuclear test ban treaty
The Beatles appear on episode of "Shindig" (ABC-TV) in the US
50 mph gust helps Robert Mitera ace 447-yd 10th hole at Miracle Hills, Omaha, Nebr to score world's longest straight hole-in-one
Charles Linster does 6,006 consecutive push-ups
Rolling Stones record four songs for their 1st live LP "Got Live If You Want It!" at a concert at Colston Hall in Bristol
Beatles turn down $1 million offer by Sid Berstein for a return performance at Shea Stadium in NYC
Rolf Hochhuth's play "Soldaten" premieres in West Berlin
Motion Picture Association of America adopts film rating system
Rioting continues in Derry, Northern Ireland after Royal Ulster Constabulary put down civil rights march two days earlier
WJMN TV channel 3 in Escanaba, MI (ABC/NBC) begins broadcasting
Terence McNally's play "Where has Tommy Flowers gone?" premieres in NYC
First NY Islanders NHL game at Nassau Coliseum; Calgary Flames win, 3-2
Iraq nationalizes Exxon and Mobil shares in Basrah Petroleum Company representing 23.75 percent equity in the company.
German Democratic Republic amends constitution
USSR adoption of the Fourth Soviet Constitution.
LA Dodgers win the pennant
USSR performs nuclear test
Cleveland Browns' Dino Hall sets club records with 9 kickoff returns

Ferrari driver Jody Scheckter retires with tyre trouble in US Grand Prix at Watkins Glen; becomes first South African to claim Formula 1 World Drivers Championship; wins by 4 points from race winner Gilles Villeneuve
USSR performs underground nuclear test
Belgium 3rd government of Martens resigns
In 1st Eastern Division championship Yanks beat Brewers 5-3
Musical "Cats" opens at Winter Garden Theater on Broadway, NYC; runs for nearly 18 years before closing on September 10, 2000, wins 7 Tony Awards and 1 Grammy
Striking umps return for Game 5 of NLCS, San Diego Padres win pennant
21st NASA Space Shuttle Mission (51-J): Atlantis 1 lands at Edwards AFB
KHQ-AM in Spokane Wash's final transmission
Lynette Woodward chosen as 1st woman in Harlem Globetrotters
PLO terrorists seize Italian cruise liner Achille Lauro
First edition of new British newspaper "Independent" published
Jim Fregosi is fired as manager of White Sox
Latvian flag raised in Riga for first time since annexation by USSR
Lou Piniella is fired as manager of Yankees for 2nd time
WNBC 660 final transmission, WFAN moves from 1050 to 660 & WUKQ begins on 1050 at 5:30 PM (NYC radio)
Israel begins handing out gas masks to its citizens
Child star Adam Rich (Eight Is Enough) arrested for stealing hypodermics
Tampa Bay Lightning become 1st NHL expansion team to win franchise opener, in  7-3 win over visiting Chicago Black Hawks
Massive Muslim demonstration in Xining, China, 12 killed
China performs nuclear test at Lop Nor, PRC
Faud Guliyev appointed premier of Azerbijan
Ingvar Carlsson forms Swedish government
Lu Bin swims female 200m medley world record (2:11.57)
Boston's Fleet Center opens, NY Islanders & Boston Bruins tie at 4-4
Mariners rally from 5-0 to force Game 5 of Division Series vs Yankees

Last competitive soccer match at Wembley Stadium, England is defeated 1-0 by Germany, with the last goal scored by Liverpool's Dietmar Hamann. Match is Tony Adams' 60th at Wembley, the record for most appearances at the stadium.
Crude oil resumes flowing through the trans-Alaska pipeline after workers welded shut a bullet hole that caused 260,000 US gallons of oil to spill out
US invasion of Afghanistan begins with an air assault and covert ground operations
Music, podcast, and video streaming service Spotify is launched by Daniel Ek and Martin Lorentzon
13 people are killed after a Sudanese military aeroplane crashes near Khartoum
ICC Men's Cricket T20 World Cup, Colombo: West Indies beat Sri Lanka by 36 runs for first title; Player of the Series: Australian all-rounder Shane Watson (249 runs • 11 wickets)
ICC Women's Cricket T20 World Cup, Colombo: Jess Cameron's 45 from 34 balls leads Australia to 142/4; beat England 138/9 to win by 4 runs to retain WC
Sébastien Loeb wins the World Rally Championship for the ninth consecutive year
James Rothman, Randy Schekman and  Thomas C. Südhof win the 2013 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine for their work on transport systems in cells
Mulatu Teshome becomes president of Ethiopia
Isamu Akasaki, Hiroshi Amano and Shuji Nakamura win the 2014 Nobel Prize in Physics for inventing blue light emitting diodes
Spanish nurse diagnosed with Ebola, the first case outside west Africa
Nobel prize in Chemistry awarded to Tomas Lindahl (Sweden), Paul Modrich (US) and Aziz Sancar (Turkey) for work on cells DNA repair
New Zealand clinch their 5th Rugby Championship with 25-24 win over South Africa at Cape Town; All Blacks undefeated in 6 games
China confirms it has detained Interpol chief Meng Hongwei, who resigns from his position after being reported missing
Jodie Whittaker debuts in her first full episode as the 13th and first female Doctor Who on BBC television
Limousine crash kills 20 people, including two pedestrians in Schoharie, New York
Romanian referendum to ban same sex marriage fails with only 20.4% voting
Nobel Prize for Medicine or Physiology awarded to Peter Ratcliffe, William Kaelin and Gregg Semenza for discovering how cells sense oxygen
Hurricane Delta makes landfall on Mexico's Yucatan Peninsula, with 100-mph winds, the 25th named storm for 2020
Nobel Prize for Chemistry awarded to Emmanuelle Charpentier, and Jennifer A. Doudna “for the development of a method for genome editing”
Sci-fi and fantasy author N. K. Jemisin receives the MacArthur "Genius" Fellowship grant
Zanzibar born writer Abdulrazak Gurnah is awarded the Nobel Prize for Literature
Nobel Peace Prize awarded jointly to Belarusian human rights advocate Ales Bialiatski, Russian human rights organisation Memorial and Ukrainian human rights organisation Center for Civil Liberties
Two 6.8 magnitude earthquakes strike Afghanistan's Herat Province, killing at least 2,400 people
Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine awarded to Victor Ambros and Gary Ruvkun "for the discovery of microRNA and its role in post-transcriptional gene regulation"
Christopher Columbusmisses Florida when he changes course
ExplorerJuan Rodríguez Cabrillo, on a voyage for Spain, is the first European to discover Catalina Island off the coast of California
Prince of OrangeFrederick Henryoccupies Breda during the Eighty Years' War
George IIIof Great Britain issues the Proclamation of 1763, closing lands in North America north and west of the Alleghenies to white settlement
NY Highlanders beat Boston Americans, 3-2 at Hilltop Park, NYC for pitcherJack Chesbro's MLB record 41st win of the season (41-12)
English nurseEdith Cavellsentenced to death along with 34 others by German court martial for running underground network to free Allied soldiers
Georgia Tech, coached byJohn Heisman, defeats Cumberland 222-0, the most lopsided score in the history of US college football
Paavo Nurmiruns world record 10 miles (50:15.0)
Ramsay MacDonaldis first British Prime Minister to address US Congress
Geramn Nazi officialsHeinrich Himmler,Rudolf HessandReinhard Heydrichinspect the concentration camp at Dachau
7th-place Brooklyn Dodgers fire managerCasey Stengel
Field MarshalErwin Rommelordered to return to Berlin
Uprising atAuschwitz-Birkenau concentration camp, Jews burn down crematoriums
Charles Ives' 2nd string quartet premieres
David Ben-Gurionforms 3rd Israeli government
First "Bandstand" broadcast in Philadelphia on WFIL-TV (Dick Clarkjoins in 1955 as a substitute-host)
Bill Veecktells St. Louis Browns stockholders he faces bankruptcy unless they drop their suit to block his move to Baltimore, they comply
PopePius XIIpublishes encyclical Ad Sinarum gentem
Beat poetAllen Ginsbergreads his poem "Howl" for the first time at a poetry reading in San Francisco.
"Pillow Talk" film directed by Michael Gordon and starringDoris DayandRock Hudsonis first released
JFKandRichard Nixon's second presidential debate
The Rolling Stonesrecord the Lennon-McCartney song "I Wanna Be Your Man" at Kingsway Sound Studio in London
"The French Connection" directed by William Friedkin and starringGene Hackman, Roy Scheider and Fernando Rey premieres in the US (Academy Awards Best Picture 1972)
Northern Ireland Prime Minister Brian Faulkner meets with British Prime MinisterEdward Heath; they agree to send an additional 1,500 British Army troops to Northern Ireland
ScotsmanJackie Stewartwins his 3rd Formula 1 World Drivers Championship despite withdrawing from the season ending US Grand Prix at Watkins Glen; wins title by 16 points fromEmerson Fittipaldi
US decidesJohn Lennonwon't be deported due to UK pot conviction
Hua Guofengofficially succeedsMao Zedongas Chairman of the Central Committee of the Communist Party of China
Guitarist Steve Hackett quits progressive rock bandGenesis
Frank Mahovlichformally retires from the NHL after a failed comeback attempt with the Detroit Red Wings
Hosni Mubarakbecomes acting-president of Egypt
Olof Palmeforms Swedish government
Walter PaytonpassesJim Brownas NFL's career rushing leader
Steve Yzermanis named captain of the Detroit Red Wings, a title he would hold for over 1,300 games
MLB Oakland A's outfielderRickey Hendersonsteals a record 8 bases in a playoff series (5 games) against Toronto Blue Jays
Law Professor Anita Hill accuses Supreme Court nomineeClarence Thomasof making sexually inappropriate comments to her
Nobel prize for literature awarded to American writerToni Morrison
Rupert Murdochlaunches Fox News with Roger Ailes as CEO
California holds gubernatorial recall election - Governor Gray Davis loses and actorArnold Schwarzeneggerbecomes governor
KingNorodom Sihanoukof Cambodia abdicates
Novaya GazetaRussian journalist Anna Politkovskaya shot and killed outside her apartment in Moscow

A digital version of psychiatristCarl Gustav Jung's 'Red Book' is published 48 years after his death and contains personal notes on his subconscious during the period in which he developed his principal theories
Peruvian authorMario Vargas Llosais awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature "for his cartography of structures of power and his trenchant images of the individual’s resistance, revolt, and defeat"
New Orleans quarterbackDrew Breesbreaks Johnny Unitas' NFL record for consecutive games with a TD pass (48) when he connects with Devery Henderson in Saints 31-24 win over San Diego at the SuperDome
US PresidentBarack Obamaapologises to Doctors without Borders President and the President of Afghanistan for the bombing of a hospital in Kunduz
Colombia's PresidentJuan Manuel Santoswins the Nobel Peace Prize for attempts to end armed conflict with FARC
Washington Post releases videotape ofDonald Trumpboasting of groping and kissing women without consent
RapperNellyarrested for rape in Auburn, Washington
Hamas launches a major air and ground attack on Israel from Gaza, killing over a thousand people and taking hundreds of hostages, prompting Prime MinisterBenjamin Netanyahuto declare "We are at war"
Battle of Lepanto: Holy League of southern European nations formed by PopePius Vdestroy an Ottoman fleet in a significant loss off Western Greece
France'sHenri Farmanflies 30m in a Voisin biplane
Far side of the Moon is seen for the first time, courtesy of the USSR's Luna 3 space probe
Elisabeth II of Bohemia. Queen consort of Hungary, born in Visegrád, Hungary (d. 1442)
Frederick I, King of Denmark and Norway (1523-33), born in Gottorp, Denmark (d. 1533)
Alessandro Farnese, Duke of Parma, Italian diplomat, Catholic cardinal, and patron of the arts, born in Valentano, Republic of Florence (d. 1589)
William Laud, English Archbishop of Canterbury (1633-45), born in Reading, Berkshire, England (d. 1645)
John Marston, English poet and playwright known for "The Malcontent", baptized in Oxfordshire (d. 1634)
Ferdinand of Bavaria, German prince elector and Catholic Archbishop of Cologne, born in Munich (d. 1650)
Maria Maddalena of Austria, Grand Duchess of Tuscany as consort of Cosimo II, born in Graz (d. 1631)
Henri Madin, French composer, born in Verdun, France (d. 1748)
Granville Elliott, British military officer (Battle of Minden), born in Barnes, England (d. 1759)
Caesar Rodney, American  judge and Founding Father (signed US Declaration of Independence, born in Dover, Delaware (d. 1784)[1]
Ralph Abercromby, British general (commanded of British forces in West Indies, North Holland and Egypt), born at Menstrie Castle, Clackmannanshire, Scotland (d. 1801)
Sergey Vyazmitinov, Russian general and statesman, born in Rylsky Uyezd, Rusiia (d. 1819)
William Billings, American hymn composer (Rose of Sharon), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1800)
Charles XIII, King of Sweden (1809-18) and King of Norway (1814-18), born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1818)
Paul Wineberger, German cellist, organist, kapellmeister, and composer, born in Mergentheim, Duchy of Württemberg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1821)
Solomon Sibley, American politician, Senator from Michigan Territory, first mayor of Detroit, born in Sutton, Massachusetts (d. 1846)
Ferdinando Orlandi, Italian musician, singing teacher, and composer, born in Parma, Duchy of Parma (d. 1848)
Louis-Joseph Papineau, Canadian lawyer and politician involved in the Patriote movement, born in Montréal, Quebec (d. 1871)

Piet Uys [Petrus Lafras Uys], Dutch Afrikaner Voortrekker pioneer (the Great Trek from Cape of Good Hope to interior South Africa), born in Potberg, Swellendam, British Cape Colony (d. 1838) [birthdate disputed, baptized on 23 Oct]
Jean-Baptiste Vuillaume, French musical instrument inventor, luthier and violin maker (Octobass), born in Mirecourt, France (d. 1875)
Adolf Muller Sr., Austrian composer of operettas, born in Tolna, Austria-Hungary (d. 1886)
Wilhelm Bernhard Molique, German violinist and composer, born in the Free Imperial City of Nuremberg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1869)
Bushrod Rust Johnson, American general in the Confederate Army, served under General Lee, born in Belmont County, Ohio (d. 1880)
Richard Heron Anderson, American general in the Confederate Army and during the Mexican-American War, born in the High Hills of Santee, South Carolina (d. 1879)
William Still, African-American abolitionist, businessman and conductor of the Underground Railroad, born in Shamong Township, New Jersey (d. 1902)
William Brimage Bate, American Brigadier-General (Confederate Army) and 23rd Governor of Tennessee (1882-86), born in Castalian Springs, Tennessee (d. 1905)
Felix Draeseke, German composer (Christus: Mysterium in a Prelude and Three Oratorios), born in Coburg, Duchy of Saxe-Coburg and Gotha (d. 1913)
Nicholas I, last and only King of Montenegro (1910-18), born in Njeguši, Montenegro (d. 1921)
James Whitcomb Riley, American author and poet (The Raggedy Man), born in Greenfield, Indiana (d. 1916)
Christiaan R de Wet, South African rebel leader, politician and general in the Boer War, born in Smithfield, Orange Free State (d. 1922)
Moses Walker, American baseball catcher (first African-American open about his heritage to play MLB; Toledo Blue Stockings), born in Mt. Pleasant, Ohio (d. 1924)
Martha McChesney Berry, American educator who founded Berry School for Children, born near Rome, Georgia (d. 1942)
Wlodimir Ledochowski, Polish-Austrian priest, director of the Society of Jesus, born in Loosdorf, Sustria-Hungary (d. 1942)
"Uncle" Dave Macon, American vaudeville and Grand Ole Opry banjo player, singer-songwriter and comedian, born in Smartt Station, Tennessee (d. 1952)
Louis Tancred, South African cricket batsman (14 Tests, 2 x 50; Transvaal), born in Port Elizabeth, South Africa (d. 1934)
Joe Hill [Joel Hägglund], Swedish-American labor activist and protest songwriter ("The Preacher and the Slave"; "There Is Power in a Union"), born in Jevla, Sweden (d. 1915)
Joseph Bovet, Swiss composer and conductor (Fribourg folk music), born in Sâles, Switzerland (d. 1951)
Jaume Pahissa, Spanish-Catalan composer (Gala Placidia; Aurora de esperanza), and musicologist, born in Barcelona, Spain (d. 1969)
Mikhail Drozdovsky, Russian general, leader of the Bolshevik White movement, born in Kiev, Russia (d. 1918)
Harold Geiger, American balloon and airplane pilot, and U.S. Army aviation pioneer, born in East Orange, New Jersey (d. 1927)
Johannes Tielrooy, Dutch literary figure (biography of Chateaubriand), born in Texel, Netherlands (d. 1953)
A.C. "Jack" Russell, English cricket batsman (10 Tests, 5 x 100s; Essex CCC), born in Leyton, England (d. 1961)
Gastone De Zuccoli, Italian organist, composer (Autunno; Messa Francescana),  and pedagogue, born in Trieste, Austria-Hungary (d. 1958)
Jack Mulhall, American actor and producer (The Three Musketeers, The Amazing Exploits of the Clutching Hand), born in Wappingers Falls, New York (d. 1979)
Cecil Coles, Scottish organist and composer (Behind the Lines), born in Tongland, Scotland (d. 1918)
Henry A. Wallace, American editor and politician (33rd US Vice-President 1941-45, founded Progressive Party), born near Orient, Iowa (d. 1965)[1]
Dwain Esper, American film director (The Seventh Commandment), born in Washington State (d. 1982)
Del Lord, Canadian director of The Three Stooges films, born in Grimsby, Ontario (d. 1970)
Herman Dooyeweerd, Dutch philosopher (suite of fifteen aspects), and legal scholar (Vrije Universiteit, 1926-65), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1977)
Cyril Allcott, New Zealand cricket all-rounder (6 Tests, 113 runs @ 22.6, 6 wickets; Auckland), born in Lower Moutere, New Zealand (d. 1973)
Alfred Wallenstein, American cellist and conductor (Chicago Symphony, 1922-29), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1983)
Frank Boucher, Canadian Hockey HOF center (Ottawa Sens, Vancouver Maroons, NY Rangers), coach (Stanley Cup 1939–40 NY Rangers) and executive (GM NY Rangers), born in Ottawa, Ontario (d. 1977)
Ralph Rainger [Reichenthal], American song and film composer ("Thanks for the Memory", "Love In Bloom"), born in New York City (d. 1942)
Souvanna Phouma, Prince and Prime Minister of Laos (1951–54, 1956–58, 1960 and 1962–75), born in Luang Phrabang, Laos (d. 1984)
Andy Devine, American character actor (Roy Roger's sidekick - "Cookie"; Stagecoach; Adventures of Wild Bill Hickok, 1951-58), born in Flagstaff, Territory of Arizona (d. 1977)
James E. Webb, American government official (Administrator of NASA, 1961-68, James Webb Space Telescope named after him), born in Tally Ho, North Carolina (d. 1992)
Helen MacInnes, Scottish-American spy writer (Agent in Place; The Salzburg Connection), born in Glasgow, Scotland (d. 1985)
Tutta Rolf, Norwegian actress (Whalers), born in Kristiania, Norway (d. 1994)
Richard Caldicot, British actor (Horse's Mouth, Strife, Mrs Thursday), born in London, England (d. 1995)
Anni Blomqvist, Finnish novelist known for the Stormskärs-Maja series, born in Vårdö, Grand Duchy of Finland, Russian Empire (d. 1990)
Erastus Corning 2nd, American politician, longest serving mayor of a major American city as Mayor of Albany (1941-83), born in Albany, New York (d. 1983)
Shura Cherkassky, Ukrainian classical pianist, born in Odesa, Russian Enpire (d. 1995)
Henry P. McIlhenny, American philanthropist and art collector, regarded as "first gentleman of Philadelphia", born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1986)
(Jonathon) "Jo" Jones, American jazz drummer, and percussionist (Count Basie), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1985)
Vaughn Monroe, American singer and orchestra leader (Vaughn Monroe Show), born in Akron, Ohio (d. 1973)
Fernando Belaúnde Terry, President of Peru (1963–1968,1980–1985), born in Lima, Peru (d. 2002)
Simon Carmiggelt, Dutch journalist (Het Volk ("The People"); Het Parool ("The Password"), and essayist (Kronkels), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1987)
Alfred Drake [Capurro], American singer and actor (Trading Places, Hamlet, Kiss Me Kate), born in Brooklyn, New York City (d. 1992)
Herman Keiser, American golfer (US Masters 1946), born in Springfield, Missouri (d. 2003)
Sarah Churchill, British actress (Matinee Theatre, All Over Town, Royal Wedding), born in London, England (d. 1982)
Margarita Aliger, Russian poet (Zoja), born in Odessa, Ukraine (d. 1992) [NS]
Roman Padlewski, Polish violinist, composer, and underground resistance fighter, born in Moscow, Russia (d. 1944)[1]
Walt Rostow, American economist and political theorist (The Stages of Economic Growth: A Non-Communist Manifesto), born in New York City (d. 2003)

June Allyson [Eleanor Geisman], American stage and screen actress (Best Foot Forward; Too Young to Kiss; The Glenn Miller Story; The DuPont Show with June Allyson), born in The Bronx, New York (d. 2006)
Guido Aristarco, Italian film critic (Cinema Nuovo), born in Fossacesia, Italy (d. 1996)
Helmut Dantine, Austrian-American actor (Shadow of the Cloak), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1982)
Marcus Klingburg, Israeli epidemiologist and soviet spy, born in Warsaw, Poland (d. 2015)
Gabriel Dell, American actor (The Bowery Boys, The Steve Allen Show), born in New York City (d. 1988)
George Duby, French medieval historian (The Three Orders: Feudal Society Imagined), born in Paris, France (d. 1996)
Zelman Cowen, Australian legal scholar (Governor General of Australia 1977-82), born in Melbourne, Australia (d. 2011)
Georg Leber, German politician (SPD), born in Obertiefenbach, Germany (d. 2012)
John Gere, English art historian, curator, and Keeper of Prints and Drawings at British Museum (1946-66), born in the United Kingdom (d. 1995)[1]
Grady Hatton, American baseball third baseman (MLB All Star 1952 Cincinnati Reds) and manager (Houston Astros 1966-68), born in Beaumont, Texas  (d. 2013)
Martha Stewart [Haworth], American actress (In A Lonely Place; Daisy Kenyon: Doll Face), born in Bardwell, Kentucky (d. 2021)
Reina Prinsen Geerligs, Dutch aspiring poet who became an anti-fascist resistance fighter, born in Semarang, Dutch East Indies (d. 1943)
Irma Grese, German Nazi concentration camp guard known as the "Hyena of Auschwitz", and convicted war criminal, born in  Wrechen, Prussia, German Republic (d. 1945)

Ivan Jirko, Czech composer, music critic and psychiatrist, born in Prague, Czechoslovakia (d. 1978)
Marcello Abbado, Italian pianist, composer, conductor, and teacher, born in Milan, Italy (d. 2020)
Al Martino [Jasper Cini], American pop singer ("Here in My Heart"), and actor (The Godfather), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2009)
R.D. Laing, Scottish psychiatrist (anti-psychiatry), and writer (The Politics of Experience and The Bird of Paradise), born in Glasgow, Scotland (d. 1989)
Reid Shelton, American stage and screen actor (Annie - "Daddy Warbucks"; 1st & 10 - "Ernie"), born in Salem, Oregon (d. 1997)
Lorna Wing (née Tolchard), English psychologist, and researcher who introduced term Asperger's Syndrome, born in Gillingham, England (d. 2014)
Sohrab Sepehri, Persian poet and painter, born in Kashan, Iran (d. 1980)
Graeme Ferguson, Canadian filmmaker, inventor. and executive (IMAX), born in Toronto, Ontario (d. 2021)
Harold Zirin, American astronomer, born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 2012)
Robert Westall, British author, born in North Shields, United Kingdom (d. 1993)
Cotton Fitzsimmons, American Basketball HOF coach (NJCAA C'ship 1966, 67, Moberly Area CC; Kansas State; NBA: Phoenix Suns; Atlanta Hawks, San Antonio Spurs), born in Hannibal, Missouri (d. 2004)
Bud Daley, American baseball pitcher (World Series 1961, 62 NY Yankees; 4 x MLB All Star KC Athletics), born in Orange, California (d. 2024)
Dick Szymanski, American football center (Pro Bowl 1955, 62, 64; Baltimore Colts) and executive (GM Baltimore Colts 1977-82), born in Toledo, Ohio (d. 2021)
Joannes M Gijsen, Bishop of Roermond, born in Oeffelt, Netherlands (d. 2013)
Paul B. Price, American actor (Busting Loose), born in Carteret, New Jersey (d. 2012)
Amiri Baraka [Everett Leroy Jones], American poet (Somebody Blew Up America), writer (Black Music), and music critic, born in Newark, New Jersey (d. 2014)
Dan Armstrong American session guitarist, and luthier, born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 2004)
Feliksas Bajoras, Lithuanian composer (Mourning Music; Exodus I), born in Alytus, Lithuania
Ulrike Meinhof, West German far left militant, born in Oldenburg, Germany (d. 1976)
Willie Naulls, American basketball small forward (NBA C'ship 1964, 65, 66 Boston Celtics; 4 × NBA All-Star; NY Knicks), born in Dallas, Texas (d. 2018)
Thomas Keneally, Australian writer (Schindler's List), born in Sydney, Australia
Charles Dutoit, Swiss conductor (Montreal Symphony, 1977-2002; Orchestre National de France. 1991-2001; Royal Philharmonic, 2009-18), born in Lausanne, Switzerland
Gennadi Mikhailovich Kolesnikov, Russian cosmonaut, born in Dauriya, Russia
Maria Szyszkowska, Polish politician, born in Warsaw, Poland
Carlos Contreras, Chilean soccer defender (30 caps; Universidad de Chile), born in Santiago, Chile (d. 2020)
Mal Lucas, Welsh soccer right-half who played 4 internationals; Leyton Orient FC, Norwich City FC, Torquay United FC, born in Wrexham, Wales (d. 2024)
Clive James, Australian television presenter, poet writer, born in Kogarah, Australia (d. 2019)
Colin Cooper, British vocalist and harmonica player (Climax Blues Band - "Couldn't Get It Right"), born in Stafford, England (d. 2008)
Harold Kroto, English chemist and Nobel laureate (1996), born in Wisbech (d. 2016)
Joel Brodsky, American photographer, created over 400 album covers (The Doors; Isaac Hayes; Ohio Players), born in Brooklyn, New York City (d. 2007)
John Hopcroft, American computer scientist, born in Seattle, Washington
